modules.title: Welcome — hydration is the real key
modules.html:
Welcome — hydration is the real key

If your Type 4 hair feels dry no matter what you buy, you are not doing it wrong — you were never taught the one thing that changes everything: hydration is water, and the skill is keeping that water in.

This course is short on theory-for-theory’s-sake and long on what to actually do. By the end you will understand why your hair dries out, and you will have a repeatable system — wash day, between washes, and at night — that keeps your hair soft for days, not hours.

Read one lesson, do it, then come back for the next. Validate each lesson to unlock the following one.

Start here Have your porosity in mind as you go (Lesson 4 shows the test). It is the single setting that personalises every step.

modules.title: Why Type 4 hair is dry — the real mechanism
modules.html:
Why Type 4 hair is dry — the real mechanism

Dry Type 4 hair is not a personal failure or a bad-genes verdict. It is physics.

Your scalp produces sebum, a natural oil that coats and protects the hair. On straight hair it slides down the strand in a day or two. On Type 4 hair, every tight coil is a hairpin bend the sebum cannot get around — so your ends, the oldest and most fragile part, are left exposed.

Two more things stack on top: each bend is a weak point where the cuticle lifts and loses water faster, and the coiled shape exposes far more surface area to dry air. More escape routes, less protection. That is the whole story.

What this means for you

You cannot out-buy biology with one miracle product. But you can do what your sebum can’t: add water deliberately, then seal it in. That is hydration — and it is a skill, not a purchase.

Reframe “Dry” is not damage. It is water that left and was never sealed back in. Everything in this course is about closing that gap.

modules.title: Moisture vs nourishment — water is not oil
modules.html:
Moisture vs nourishment — water is not oil

This is the confusion that keeps most people stuck. Grease is not moisture. Only water hydrates.

Reach for oil when hair feels dry and it seems to help — for an hour. But oil is a sealant, not a moisturiser. If there is no water underneath, you are sealing dryness in. That is why hair can feel greasy and parched at the same time.

Moisture = water

Water and water-based products (leave-in, aloe, a hydrating spritz). This is what softens and plumps the strand.

Nourishment = oil / butter

Oils and butters (shea, jojoba, castor). These don’t add water — they slow its escape. Always second, never instead.

The golden order

Water first, oil second. Hydrate on damp hair, then lock it with a light oil or cream. Reverse the order and nothing gets in. Get this one idea right and half your dryness problem is already solved.

Quick test If your “moisturiser” has no water (aqua) near the top of the label, it is a sealant. Great for step two — useless as step one.

modules.title: Your porosity — the setting that personalises everything
modules.html:
Your porosity — the setting that personalises everything

Porosity is how easily water gets into — and out of — your hair. It decides which products and methods actually work for you.

The strand test

1

Take a clean strand of shed hair (no product on it).

2

Drop it in a glass of room-temperature water.

3

Watch for 2–3 minutes — don’t poke it.

Low porosity

Floats a long time. Tight cuticle: water struggles to get in and to leave. Product tends to sit on top. Your ally is gentle heat and lighter textures.

High porosity

Sinks quickly. Open cuticle: drinks water in fast, loses it just as fast. Your ally is sealing and heavier creams/butters.

Most Type 4 hair leans a little high, especially with any colour or heat history — but test, don’t assume. Some strands sit in the middle (medium/normal): they behave in-between and are the most forgiving.

Remember this Every recipe and step in the Studio has a low- and high-porosity note. Now you know which one is yours — read that box first, every time.

modules.title: LOC & LCO — the retention method
modules.html:
LOC & LCO — the retention method

LOC and LCO are just a reliable order for layering products so the water actually stays. Three steps.

1

L — Liquid/Leave-in: water or a water-based leave-in on damp hair. This is the hydration.

2

O — Oil: a light oil to slow water loss.

3

C — Cream: a cream or butter to soften and seal the surface.

LOC and LCO use the same three players in a different order — and porosity decides which:

Low porosity → LCO

Cream before oil. Your tight cuticle rejects heavy oil first; a lighter cream layer goes on better, then a little oil on top.

High porosity → LOC

Oil before cream. Your open cuticle needs the oil to trap water quickly, then the cream to cap it all off.

Don’t overthink the letters. The non-negotiable is the first step: start on damp hair with something water-based. The rest is just locking that in.

Less is more Layering ≠ piling on. Thin layers, section by section, beat one thick greasy coat that never absorbs.

modules.title: Sealing — the move that keeps water in
modules.html:
Sealing — the move that keeps water in

Hydrating without sealing is like filling a bath with the plug out. Sealing is the plug.

Right after you add water and leave-in, your hair is at its softest — and its most likely to lose that water within the hour. A thin layer of oil, cream or butter over the top slows evaporation dramatically. That is the entire job of “sealing”.

What to seal with

Lighter seals

Jojoba, grapeseed, sweet almond, a light hair milk. For low porosity and fine strands — they hold without weighing down.

Heavier seals

Shea butter, castor oil, thick creams. For high porosity, thick or very dry ends — they need the extra barrier.

Focus sealing on your ends. They are the oldest, driest, most exposed part — protect them first and length retention follows.

Timing Seal on damp hair, not soaking, not dry. Soaking = the oil slides off water it can’t hold; bone-dry = there was nothing to seal in.

modules.title: The hydrating wash day
modules.html:
The hydrating wash day

Wash day is where hydration is won or lost. Done right, it sets you up for a soft week.

1

Pre-poo (optional): oil or conditioner on dry hair 20–30 min to protect it from the strip of washing.

2

Cleanse gently: a sulfate-free shampoo on the scalp, or a co-wash. Never scrub the lengths.

3

Deep condition: 20–30 min with a moisture mask — low porosity add heat. This is your big weekly water recharge.

4

Rinse cool: a final cool rinse helps close the cuticle and hold shine.

5

Leave-in on soaking-damp hair: section by section, roots-safe, ends-focused.

6

Seal (LOC/LCO): lock it with oil/cream per your porosity.

7

Style low-stress: a twist-out, braids or a puff that won’t need daily re-doing.

Low porosity

Warmth is your friend — deep condition with a cap and gentle heat, and keep every layer light so it absorbs.

High porosity

Lead with moisture and seal firmly — consider a leave-in + cream + a butter on the ends to hold the week.

Frequency Most Type 4 hair thrives on washing every 5–10 days. Too often strips you dry; too rarely lets buildup block moisture. Find your rhythm.

modules.title: Between washes — do I hydrate every day?
modules.html:
Between washes — do I hydrate every day?

The honest answer: no, not necessarily. You re-moisturise when your hair asks for it — and a good seal means it asks less often.

If you sealed well on wash day, many people go 2–3 days (or more) before a refresh. Chasing a daily soak-and-seal can actually over-wet fine or low-porosity hair and weigh it down. Let your hair set the pace.

The 30-second refresh

1

Lightly mist with water or a hydrating spritz (aloe + water + a drop of oil).

2

Smooth a pea of leave-in or cream over the ends only.

3

Fluff or re-shape; re-tie if needed. Done.

Low porosity

Refresh less, and lighter — a fine mist, minimal cream. Too much sits on top and turns greasy, not hydrated.

High porosity

Refresh a little more often and always re-seal the ends — your hair lets water go faster, so top it up and cap it.

How to tell it’s thirsty Ends feel rough, hair sounds crunchy, coils look dull and won’t clump. That’s the cue — not the calendar.

modules.title: Night & long-term retention
modules.html:
Night & long-term retention

You lose more moisture to your cotton pillowcase and to friction than you think. Protecting hair at night is half the battle.

The non-negotiables

1

Satin or silk — a bonnet, scarf, or pillowcase. Cotton drinks your hydration and roughs up the cuticle.

2

Loose “pineapple” or big twists — gather hair up loosely, or twist in a few large sections to keep moisture and definition.

3

Don’t re-wet every night — a light refresh only if needed; over-manipulation is what dries and breaks hair.

Over weeks, the biggest lever is low-manipulation styles: twists, braids, buns, or a protective style worn for days. Every time you leave hair alone, moisture stays and ends survive. Retention is mostly about not undoing your own work.

Low porosity

A light night — minimal product, satin cover. Reapplying nightly makes low-po hair limp and greasy.

High porosity

Re-seal the ends before bed some nights, and lean on longer protective styles — they hold water in between wash days.

Length is retained, not grown fast Your hair grows about the same as anyone’s. Type 4 “doesn’t grow” only because dry ends snap. Keep ends hydrated and sealed and you keep your length.

modules.title: Climate, seasons & the mistakes that dry you out
modules.html:
Climate, seasons & the mistakes that dry you out

The same routine behaves differently in Abidjan and in a heated winter flat. And a few common habits quietly undo all your effort.

Climate & glycerin

Glycerin and honey are humectants — they pull water from the air into your hair. In humidity (tropics, summer) they’re brilliant. In very dry or cold air, they can pull water out of your hair instead. Match your products to your weather, not to a video filmed on the other side of the world.

Humid / hot

Humectants shine (glycerin, aloe, honey). Lighter seals. Your hair holds water more easily — enjoy it.

Dry / cold

Ease off humectants; lean on richer creams and butters and a firmer seal to defend against the dry air.

The dryness traps

  • Sulfate shampoo every wash — strips the little protection you have.
  • Oiling dry hair and calling it hydration (Lesson 3).
  • Hard water buildup — a monthly clarify or ACV rinse resets it.
  • Too much protein, no moisture — hard, snappy hair. Balance the two.
  • “Drying” alcohols high on a label (alcohol denat.) in leave-ins.
  • Daily heat, cotton at night, and over-manipulation.
One change at a time Don’t fix all six at once. Swap one habit, watch your hair for two weeks, then the next. That’s how you learn your hair.

modules.title: Your 4-week hydration plan + cheat sheet
modules.html:
Your 4-week hydration plan

Knowledge only helps if it becomes a rhythm. Here is a gentle 4-week on-ramp — adjust the wash frequency to your hair.

  • Week 1 — Reset: clarify or ACV rinse once, then a full hydrating wash day (Lesson 7). Do the porosity test. Note how your hair feels.
  • Week 2 — Method: wash day + practise LOC/LCO for your porosity. One 30-second refresh midweek. Switch to satin at night if you haven’t.
  • Week 3 — Retention: wash day + a low-manipulation style worn 3–4 days. Refresh only when hair asks. Seal ends nightly if high porosity.
  • Week 4 — Personalise: wash day + adjust one thing (lighter/richer seal, more/less refresh) based on what weeks 1–3 taught you. This is now your routine.

Cheat sheet — “my hair is dry when…”

…it feels rough by day 2

You under-sealed. Add a heavier seal on the ends, or a midweek refresh + re-seal.

…it’s greasy but still dry

You oiled without water. Go back to water-first, then a lighter seal (Lesson 3).

…it’s dry only at the ends

Normal — ends are oldest. Focus sealing there; trim if they’re split and won’t hold moisture.

…it’s dry everywhere, fast

Likely high porosity + dry air. Richer creams, firmer seal, ease off humectants, clarify buildup.

You’ve got this Hydration isn’t a product you find — it’s a habit you keep. Come back to this course whenever your hair changes with the seasons. 💛
